coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots activated metal organic frameworks for selective detection of Cu(Ⅱ) and Fe(Ⅲ)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Lu
Wang, Yingxi
Li, Ling
Zhou, Jianggang
coredata.dc:description: In this study, 2-amino-terephthalic acid was designed for construction of MOFs, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were designed to activate the Metal Organic Frameworks (MOFs) to be used as fluorescent sensor for selectively detection for Fe3+ and Cu2+, enhancing the fluorescence to an “on” state by the hybrid with CQDs in the MOFs as activating agent; quenching the fluorescence to an “off” state by removal of CQDs through the metal competitive coordination substitution strategy. Using this strategy, the prepared CQDs/MOFs material can be used as a fluorescence “on-off” probe for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ in aqueous solution. It was found that the LOD of CQDs/MOFs for sensing Fe3+ and Cu2+ were 2.3 ppb and 1.3 ppb, respectively. It was proved that the CQDs/MOFs has little cytotoxicity to 4T-1 cells, indicating the potential application in the detection for Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions in cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
MOFs
Fluorescence probe
Selectively
Metal ions

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Luminescence of the MOFs and the CQDs/MOFs
Luminescent measurements for sensing metal ions
Detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ by using CQDs/MOFs
Luminescence of the MOFs and CQDs/MOFs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM patterns of CQDs(a,b) and CQDs/MOFs(c,d).

Luminescence emission spectra of the CQDs, MOFs and CQDs/MOFs in water solution with excitation at 380 nm (a) and quenching effect for sensing metal ions (100 ppm) of CQDs (b), MOFs (c) and CQDs/MOFs (d). The excitation slit are 5 nm and emission slit is 3 nm.
Luminescence emission spectra of CQDs/MOFs dispersed in water solution after adding different concentration of Cu2+(a) and different concentration of Fe3+(b).
Linear plots of the quenching coefficient of CQDs/MOFs in water solution for sensing Cu2+ (a,c) and Fe3+ (b,c) in the range of 0−10 ppm (a,b) and 10−100 ppm(c,d).
Coexisting ion interference of CQDs/MOFs for luminescent sensing Cu2+ (a) and Fe3+ (b).
Cell viability of 4T-1 cells exposed to CQDs/MOFs nanoparticles at various concentrations.
Synthetic route for CQDs/MOFs and the turn-off sensor for sensing Cu(Ⅱ) based on the CQDs/MOFs.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots activated metal organic frameworks for selective detection of Cu(Ⅱ) and Fe(Ⅲ)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Function Molecules, Hubei University, 430062, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Function Molecules

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: a. Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Function Molecules, Hubei University, 430062, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Resources and Environmental Science, Hubei University, 430062, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Resources and Environmental Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: b. Faculty of Resources and Environmental Science, Hubei University, 430062, People’s Republic of China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, 2-amino-terephthalic acid was designed for construction of MOFs, and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were designed to activate the Metal Organic Frameworks (MOFs) to be used as fluorescent sensor for selectively detection for Fe3+ and Cu2+, enhancing the fluorescence to an “on” state by the hybrid with CQDs in the MOFs as activating agent; quenching the fluorescence to an “off” state by removal of CQDs through the metal competitive coordination substitution strategy. Using this strategy, the prepared CQDs/MOFs material can be used as a fluorescence “on-off” probe for highly sensitive detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ in aqueous solution. It was found that the LOD of CQDs/MOFs for sensing Fe3+ and Cu2+ were 2.3 ppb and 1.3 ppb, respectively. It was proved that the CQDs/MOFs has little cytotoxicity to 4T-1 cells, indicating the potential application in the detection for Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions in cells.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of modern industrial technology, water pollution has become increasingly serious. Among others, heavy metal ions are the main water pollutants because of their wide applications in many fields such as mining, chemical manufacturing, tanning, battery manufacturing and other industries [1–3]. They can easily bind to important cellular components, accumulate in organs and cause serious diseases, disorders and organ failure [4,5].

Fe3+, as an important metal center of catalysis and biotechnology, plays a vital role in biology, usually involved in the formation of hemoglobin and DNA synthesis [6]. Proper concentration of Fe3+ intake can prevent certain diseases such as heart failure, endotoxin, pancreas, cirrhosis, Parkinson's and Alzheimer's disease [7,8].

Cu2+ is essential to plants and animals [9,10]. The lack of Cu2+ will lead to diseases such as anemia, osteoporosis and vitiligo [11]. If it exceeds the standard in human body, it may cause diseases such as cirrhosis, diarrhea and vomiting [12].

Since the selective detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ is of great significance to human health, developing a method for rapidly identifying and detecting metal ions has become a hot issue. Traditional analytical techniques, such as mass spectrometry (MS) [13],

atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [14] and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) [15] and so on, are difficult to meet the needs of the real-time detection because of the complication and time-consuming. With the development of research, the method using fluorescent sensors shows outstanding properties, such as simple operation, low cost, short response time, and highly sensitivity. In the application of method using fluorescent sensors, it is important to design a fluorescent material.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) not only display versatile structural tunability and functionality, but also have been explored for luminescence applications. As fluorescence sensors, MOFs can be synthesized by selecting different ligands and metal ions in order to selectively detect different heavy metal ions. Eu3+ doped MIL-53−COOH(Al) was synthesized by Zhou to detect Fe3+ in an aqueous [16]. Since the detection is based on cation exchange mechanism, the detection process takes relative long time. Senthilkumar synthesized pore wall functionalized luminescent Cd(II) framework for selective detecting and sensing of Cu2+ ions [17], however, this work has a lower detection limit for Cu2+ ions.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted great attention due to their excellent luminescence properties [18] and low-toxicity [19]. According to the restriction of intramolecular rotation principle [20], rigid linkers in MOFs can efficiently and substantially tune the electronic transition energies and enhance the fluorescence, thus CQDs can activate the fluorescence of MOFs, offering great opportunities for MOFs to become fluorescence probes.

Inspired by this, CQDs is used to activate the fluorescence of MOFs in this work. Through the hydrogen bonding between hydroxyl group of CQDs and amino group of MOFs, CQDs is Doped into MOFs to form CQDs/MOFs to produce enhanced fluorescence. In the presence of Cu2+ and Fe3+, CQD is detached from MOFs, resulting in fluorescence quenching because this is due to the stronger coordination between the metal ion and the amino group of the MOF than the hydrogen bond. The competition results exhibit an“on-off” switching in the presence of trace metal ions.

Glucan, 2-amino-terephthalic acid (2-NH2BDC) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Zinc nitrate, copper nitrate, ferric chloride, lead nitrate, cadmium chloride, manganese chloride, magnesium sulfate, nickel nitrate, aluminum sulfate, cobalt nitrate, chromium nitrate and sodium nitrate were purchased from Sinopharm (Shanghai) Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. All other chemicals used in this work were of analytical grade. Fresh double-distilled water was used in all experiments.

Powder X-ray diffractometry (XRD) patterns were obtained using a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker Company, Germany). Fourier Transform Infra-Red (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Spectrum One FT-IR spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA) at room temperature. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on a Tecnai G20 microscope (FEI, America). The fluorescence measurements were recorded on LS55 fluorescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, America).

CQDs was synthesized by a modified hydrothermal methods [21]. Typically, 0.24 g glucan was dissolved in 30 mL water, and the solution was then transferred into the Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 160℃ for 24 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was dialyzed in distilled water for 48 h. The CQDs were obtained.

MOFs was prepared according to a literature procedure with some modifications [22]. Briefly, 0.446 g Zn(NO3)2·6H2O was ultrasonically dispersed in 10 mL DMF for 30 min and designated as solution A. 0.091 g 2-NH2BDC was ultrasonically dispersed in 15 mL DMF for 30 min and designated as solution B. Solution A and solution B were homogeneously mixed and then transferred to a Teflon autoclave for reaction at 110℃ for 24 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the resulting crystals were washed with ethanol and water several times, respectively, dried for 10 h at 80℃. The MOFs were obtained for further use.

The CQDs/MOFs was prepared according to a literature procedure [23] with some modifications. Typically, 0.446 g Zn(NO3)2·6H2O was ultrasonically dispersed in 10 mL DMF and designated as solution A, then 4 mL CQDs solution was added. 0.091 g 2-NH2BDC was ultrasonically dispersed in 15 mL DMF and designated as solution B. The mixture was heated at 110 °C for 24 h. The resulting crystals were washed several times with ethanol and water and dried at 80 °C for 10 h to obtain CQDs/MOFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence of the MOFs and the CQDs/MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: 1.0 mg MOFs and 1.0 mg CQDs/MOFs were added into 10 mL water solution and ultrasonicated until completely dispersed, respectively. Then suspensions of MOFs and CQDs/MOFs were prepared. The luminescence emission spectra of MOFs and CQDs/MOFs suspensions were obtained by exciting at 380 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescent measurements for sensing metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: NaNO3, Cu(NO3)2, CdCl2, Cr(NO3)3, MgSO4, FeCl3, Zn(NO3)2, MnCl2 and Al2(SO4)3 were used to prepare the solutions of different metal ions. Metal ion solutions (100 ppm) were prepared in double-distilled water. Suspensions of the CQDs/MOFs (100 ppm) were prepared by dispersing CQDs/MOFs in water solution and ultrasonicated until completely dispersed. The luminescence of the synthesized CQDs/MOFs in water solution was investigated at room temperature. To explore the luminescent sensing properties of the CQDs/MOFs for detecting metal ions, the luminescence spectra of the CQDs/MOFs suspensions (100 ppm) were recorded by adding a series of metal ions (Na+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Zn2+ and Mn2+) with the same concentration (100 ppm). To further explore the selectivity of the luminescent CQDs/MOFs for sensing metal ions, the luminescence spectra of the CQDs/MOFs suspensions (100 ppm) were measured before and after adding other metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ by using CQDs/MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: 1.0 mg CQDs/MOFs was added into 10 mL water solution and then oscillated for 10 min to obtain CQDs/MOFs suspension. 1 mL CQDs/MOFs suspension was added into the water solution with different concentration of Fe3+. The above solution was oscillated for 5 min to ensure the complete reaction between Fe3+ and CQDs/MOFs before detection. For the selective sensing experiments, the concentrations of interfering ions and Fe3+ were 100 ppm, respectively. Fluorescence emission spectra were obtained by exciting at 380 nm. The detection of Cu2+ is with the same method.

Mouse breast cancer (4T1) cells were cultured in standard cell media into 96-well plates and incubated in 5 % CO2 at 37 ℃ for 24 h. Then different concentrations of particles were added into the 96-well plates and continued to be cultured for 24 h. CKK-8 assay was used to analyze cell viability. Typically, the cells were incubated for 24 h with 100 mL CQDs/MOFs in PBS buffer (25, 50, 100, 200, 500 and 1000 μg/mL). Cells were rinsed with PBS for three times and then each well was added to 100 μL of 0.1 mg/mL CKK-8 solution and incubated for 30 min at 37 ℃. Finally, an ELISA readers was also used to measure the absorbance at 480 nm of each well. Results were expressed as the percentage of cell viability.

The TEM images of CQDs and CQDs/MOFs are shown in Fig. 1 . Fig.1(a) and (b) show that the CQDs particles are with uniform 10 nm size. MOFs particles present the structure of the tetragonal phase, with uniform 100 nm size, as shown in Fig. 1(c). From Fig. 1(d), it can be seen that the spherical CQDs aggregates between the MOF particles appear (Fig. 1(d)), indicating the successful synthesis of CQDs/MOFs. The small size of CQD/MOFs ensures good stability and dispersion in aqueous solution to improve the stability of fluorescence detection.

The confirmation of CQDs/MOFs can be further proved by XRD patterns and FT-IR spectra. Fig. 2 (a) shows the powder XRD pattern of MOFs and CQDs/MOFs. The peaks of MOFs at 10.2°, 12.1°, 16.1°, 19.5°, 20.7° and 23.2°, which are in good agreement with the reported one [24]. In comparison, the diffraction peaks of CQDs/MOFs are similar to those of MOFs, indicating the structure of MOFs is preserved. The wide peak of CQDs at 23° becomes sharp and weak in CQDs/MOFs, indicating the CQDs component is affected by MOFs in composite[25]. In addition three new peaks at 9°, 14° and 16° appear in CQDs/MOFs, suggesting that CQDs/MOFs is rather than a simple physical mix. It is very likely that a new lattice structure is formed. All these prove the successful synthesis of CQDs/MOFs.

The FT-IR spectra of CQDs/MOFs is shown in Fig. 2(b). Compared 2-NH2BDC with MOFs, the peak at 3500 cm−1 of −OH of 2-NH2BDC is weakened in MOFs. The peaks at 3370 cm−1 and 3260 cm−1 of 2-NH2BDC are attributed to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of NH2, which shit to 3310 cm−1 and 3150 cm−1 in MOFs. The peak at 1690 cm−1 is assigned to stretching vibration of CO, which shifts to 1610 cm-1 in MOFs. All these can be explained by the coordination between the O atom of 2-NH2BDC and Zn2+. The peak at 3600 cm−1 of CQDs is attributed to the OH stretching vibration, which shifts to 3450 cm−1 in CQDs/MOFs; 3310 cm−1 and 3150 cm−1 of MOFs shift a little to 3260 cm−1 and 3200 cm−1. The shift of the characteristic peaks of hydroxyl group and amino group indicates the hydrogen bonding between the groups. It also proves that CQDs/MOFs is not simple physical mix of CQDs and MOFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence of the MOFs and CQDs/MOFs
ce:section.ce:para: The luminescence emission spectra of the ligand 2-NH2BDC, MOFs, CQDs, CQDs/MOFs with 100 mg/L is shown in Fig. 3 (a). It can be seen there is a fluorescence peak of 2-NH2BDC at 460 nm. The fluorescence is derived from the conjugation effect of the aromatic ring, and hydroxyl group is an electron donating group which enhanced the conjugation effect of π electrons. After formation of MOFs by coordination between Zn2+ and hydroxyl group, the conjugation effect is weakened, leading to the decrease of fluorescence intensity. Due to the steric hindrance caused by the coordination of the amino groups, the conjugated system is attenuated, resulting in a blue shift to 425 nm of the fluorescent peak.

The fluorescence peak of CQDs at 450 nm is derived from π-π* transition of “CC” group in its structure. After the formation of CQDs/MOFs, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs/MOFs at 430 nm is much stronger than that of MOFs, and the fluorescence peak exhibits a significant red-shift. It can be explained by the hydrogen bonding between the carboxyl group on the surface of CQDs and amino group of MOFs, to enhance the conjugate chain, thereby enhancing the conjugation of π electrons to sensitize the luminescence of MOFs [26].

In order to investigate the possibility of CQDs/MOFs as a fluorescence probe, the effect of different metal ions (Na+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Fe3+, Mg2+, Zn2+ and Mn2+) on the fluorescence intensity are discussed, as shown in Fig. 3(b–d).The concentration of metal ions was 100 ppm, and all metal ions were detected under identical environment conditions. Fig. 3(b) shows that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs is not affected by addition of different metal ions, indicating that CQDs cannot be used as a fluorescence probe. Fig. 3(c) shows that the fluorescence is decreased only in the presence of Cu2+, and is not affected in the presence of other metal ions. It can be explained by the coordination between Cu2+ and nitrogen atom of MOFs. In comparison, the fluorescence of CQDs/MOFs is quenched in the presence of Cu2+. Because coordination between metal ions and nitrogen atom is stronger than the hydrogen bonding between hydroxyl group of CQDs and amino group of MOFs, CQDs will be detached from the MOFs, resulting in an decrease in the conjugation effect of π electrons. Therefore, the fluorescence is quenched by Cu2+ greatly, as shown in Scheme 1 . Based on the above analysis, due to the greater decrease of fluorescence intensity of CQDs/MOFs after the addition of metal ions, CQDs/MOFs is more suitable as fluorescent probe to detect metal ions than MOFs. Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity have no change after adding Na+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Mg2+, Zn2+ and Mn2+, while the fluorescence is quenched both in the presence of Cu2+ and Fe3 + as shown in Fig. 3(d). It indicates that CQDs/MOFs possesses extremely high selectivity for sensing Fe3+ and Cu2+. As a result, CQDs/MOFs is an excellent candidate for selective detection of metal ions by luminescence turn-off effect.

To explore the sensitivity of luminescent CQDs/MOFs for detecting Cu2+ and Fe3+, the luminescence spectra of CQDs/MOFs after adding different concentrations of Cu2+ and Fe3+ are measured, as shown in Fig. 4 . The luminescence intensity apparently increases with increasing concentration of Cu2+ and Fe3+. Furthermore, the degree of decrease is quantitatively related to the concentration of metal ions.

When the concentration of Cu2+/Fe3+ in a range from 0 to 10 ppm, the luminescent quenching effect is qualitatively examined, as shown in Fig. 5 (a) and (b). There is a good linear relationship between the decreased intensity (I0-I) and the concentration of metal ions [27]. When the concentration of Cu2+/Fe3+ in a range from 10 to 100 ppm, the luminescent quenching effect is qualitatively examined, as shown in Fig. 5(c) and (d). There is a linear relationship between the I0/I and the concentration [28–30]. On basis of the linear relationship between the decrease intensity and the concentration of metal ions in the low concentration, a method for determining trace amount of Cu2+/Fe3+ can be established. The limit of detection concentration (LOD) of the CQDs/MOFs for sensing Cu2+ and Fe3+ is calculated by using 3δ/k (k: slope, δ: standard deviation, in this method, δ = 0.0094). The LODs of CQDs/MOFs for sensing Cu2+ and Fe3+ was 1.3 ppb and 2.3 ppb, respectively. The detection limit is lower than that using bare CQDs as probe [27–29]. Therefore, CQDs/MOFs can be used as a fluorescence sensor for sensing Cu2+ and Fe3+.

In practical applications, the high selectivity is also an important factor for luminescent sensors. Therefore, we performed a series of competition experiments by measuring the luminescent spectra of CQDs/MOFs after adding given concentration (100 ppm) of Na+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Al3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, then Cu2+ or Fe3+ is added in the presence of other above metal ions, respectively. It is worth noting that only CQDs/MOFs fluorescence can be quenched by Cu2+ or Fe3+. In contrast, other metal ions do not affect the fluorescence of CQDs/MOFs. However, as long as Cu2+ or Fe3+ is added simultaneously to these metal ions, the fluorescence of the CQDs/MOFs sensor is immediately turned off, as shown in Fig. 6 .

It is clear that the coexisting metal ions doesn’t affect the fluorescence quenching of CQDs/MOFs caused by the addition of Cu2+/Fe3+. It indicates that CQDs/MOFs possesses extremely high selectivity for luminescent sensing Cu2+/Fe3+ even in presence of other coexisting metal ions.

The cytotoxicity of the CQDs/MOFs nanoparticles on 4T-1 cells is examined via the MTS cell viability assay, as shown in Fig. 7 . The CQDs/MOFs has little cytotoxicity to 4T-1 cells after incubation for 24 h. As the concentration of the CQDs/MOFs increasing to 1000 μg/mL, the cell activity remains 100 %. These results demonstrate that the CQDs/MOFs nanoparticles do not significantly impact the cell viability, indicating CQDs/MOFs has potential applications in detecting Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions in cells.

In this research, CQDs/MOFs is synthesized and characterized. Compared with MOFs, the fluorescence of CQDs/MOFs is enhanced and shows a red-shit, proving that CQDs can activate the fluorescence of MOFs. After the addition of Cu2+ and Fe3+, CQDs/MOFs shows a luminescence turn-off behavior, and thus is used as a fluorescence “on-off” probe for selectively detecting Cu2+ and Fe3+. The mechanic can be explained by the fact that since the coordination of the metal ions with the organic ligands in MOFs is stronger than the hydrogen bonding between MOFs and CQDs, CQDs are released after the addition of the metal ions, resulting in the quenching of the activated fluorescence of MOFs. The detection method using this kind of “on -off” probe, with LOD of 2.3 ppb and 1.3 ppb for Fe3+ and Cu2+, is more sensitive than the method using bare CQDs as a fluorescence probe [26–28]. Through the fluorescence activation of CQDs and the competition mechanism between coordination and hydrogen bonding, CQDs / MOFs shows strong selectivity and sensitivity for detecting metal ions as a on-off fluorescent probe. It indicates that based on the coordination, different organic ligands can be selected to design different MOFs, and "on-off" fluorescent probes can be formed through the combination of CQDs, which are expected to detect different metal ions sensitively. Furthermore, cytotoxicity of the CQDs/MOFs nanoparticles on 4T-1 cells shows that the CQDs/MOFs has little cytotoxicity to cells, suggesting the CQDs/MOFs can be not only used to detect Cu2+/Fe3+ ions in environmental drinking water, but also to detect Cu2+/Fe3+ ions in cells, which is hopefully used for the detection and prevention of diseases caused by abnormal concentration of Cu2+/Fe3+.
Lu Fan： Synthesis and fluorescence Experiments
Yingxi Wang: Finish all kinds of Characterization
Ling Li: Tutor of Lu Fan, responsible of the design of the project, and guide the experiments.
Jianggang Zhou: Guidance for some experiments.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Hubei province (No. 2017CFB530), and Wuhan Morning Light Plan of Youth Science and Technology (No. 2017050304010282). This work was supported by Ministry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Synthesis and Application of Organic Function Molecules, Hubei University.
